title: Diffusion — how substances spread out
examTip: Always describe diffusion as movement from HIGH to LOW concentration — and say it's passive (no energy needed). Be ready to explain how adaptations like the alveoli maximise the rate.
introTips: This lesson explains why substances like oxygen and glucose move into your cells without your body having to do any work.
You need to know the definition precisely, the four factors that affect the rate, and real examples from the body.
AQA often asks you to apply diffusion to a given scenario — alveoli and villi come up most frequently.
intro: Imagine dropping a dye tablet into a glass of water. You don't stir it. You don't heat it. But slowly, the colour spreads out until it's evenly distributed throughout the glass. That's diffusion. Your cells do the same thing — continuously, automatically, without using a drop of energy. Oxygen drifts into your blood. Carbon dioxide drifts out. It's one of the most important processes in your body, and it costs nothing.

keyPoints.heading: What diffusion is
keyPoints.content: Diffusion is the net movement of particles from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration — down the concentration gradient. It is a passive process — no energy is required. Particles move randomly, but there are more collisions heading from the crowded side to the less-crowded side, so the net movement is always down the gradient. Eventually, equilibrium is reached — particles keep moving, but there's no longer a net direction.
writeBullets: Diffusion = net movement of particles from high concentration to low concentration
Direction: DOWN the concentration gradient
Passive process — no energy (ATP) required
Continues until equilibrium is reached (concentrations equalise)
Applies to gases and dissolved substances

keyTerms.def: The net movement of particles from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration — a passive process requiring no energy
The difference in concentration of a substance between two areas — the steeper the gradient, the faster the rate of diffusion
A process that does not require energy (ATP) — it happens as a result of random particle movement

keyPoints.memTip: The definition has three parts: NET movement, HIGH to LOW, PASSIVE. Write it out three times in full. In the exam, every part earns marks — 'particles move' on its own is not enough. 'Net movement of particles from high to low concentration, requiring no energy' is the target sentence.

keyPoints.heading: What affects the rate of diffusion
keyPoints.content: Four factors change how fast diffusion happens. Concentration gradient: a bigger difference between the two sides means faster diffusion. Surface area: more surface area means more space for particles to cross — faster. Temperature: higher temperature gives particles more energy, so they move faster — rate increases. Thickness of the membrane: a thinner membrane means a shorter distance to travel — faster. Remember: all four can be increased to speed diffusion up, and reduced to slow it down.
writeBullets: Bigger concentration gradient → faster diffusion
Larger surface area → faster diffusion
Higher temperature → faster diffusion (particles have more kinetic energy)
Thinner membrane → faster diffusion (shorter distance to cross)
Smaller surface area / thicker membrane / smaller gradient / lower temperature all slow diffusion

keyTerms.def: The total area available for diffusion across a membrane — larger surface area increases diffusion rate
Energy of movement — higher temperature increases particle kinetic energy, increasing diffusion rate

keyPoints.memTip: Four factors: CAST — Concentration gradient, Area (surface area), Surface thickness (membrane thickness), Temperature. All four: BIGGER/LARGER/HIGHER/THINNER = faster. Flip each one for slower. One word per factor in the exam is not enough — say 'larger surface area' and 'increases the rate of diffusion'.

keyPoints.heading: Diffusion in the body
keyPoints.content: Diffusion is happening all over your body right now. In the lungs: oxygen diffuses from the air in the alveoli into the blood (high O₂ in air, low O₂ in blood); carbon dioxide diffuses in the opposite direction (high CO₂ in blood, low CO₂ in exhaled air). In body tissues: oxygen diffuses from the blood into cells; CO₂ diffuses out of cells into the blood. In the small intestine: glucose and amino acids diffuse from the gut into the blood after digestion. Urea — a waste product — diffuses out of cells into the blood to be filtered by the kidneys.
writeBullets: Lungs: O₂ diffuses from alveoli into blood; CO₂ diffuses from blood into alveoli
Body tissues: O₂ diffuses from blood into cells; CO₂ diffuses from cells into blood
Small intestine: glucose and amino acids diffuse from gut into blood
Urea diffuses from cells into blood to be removed by kidneys
Direction always: high concentration → low concentration

keyTerms.def: Tiny air sacs in the lungs where gas exchange (diffusion of O₂ and CO₂) takes place
A waste product of protein breakdown that diffuses from cells into the blood and is excreted by the kidneys

keyPoints.memTip: For each example, always state the direction — don't just say 'oxygen diffuses in the lungs', say 'oxygen diffuses FROM the alveoli INTO the blood'. Examiners want to see that you know which way the gradient runs. The quick test: which side has more of the substance? That's where diffusion starts.

keyPoints.heading: Adaptations for diffusion — alveoli and villi
keyPoints.content: Surfaces where lots of diffusion needs to happen are adapted to maximise the rate. Alveoli (lungs) have: a large surface area (millions of tiny sacs), thin walls (one cell thick — short diffusion distance), and a rich blood supply (maintains steep concentration gradient). Villi (small intestine) have: a large surface area (finger-like projections, covered in microvilli), thin walls (one cell thick), and a good blood supply (carries absorbed nutrients away, maintaining the gradient). Both adaptations exist for the same reason: steeper gradient, bigger area, shorter distance = maximum rate.
writeBullets: Alveoli adaptations: large surface area, thin walls (one cell thick), rich blood supply
Villi adaptations: large surface area (finger-like folds + microvilli), thin walls, good blood supply
Rich/good blood supply matters because it removes the diffused substance, maintaining the concentration gradient
Thin walls = short diffusion distance = faster rate
Both adaptations increase rate by maximising three of the four factors

keyTerms.def: Finger-like projections lining the small intestine that increase surface area for absorption by diffusion
Tiny projections on the surface of villi cells that further increase the surface area available for absorption

keyPoints.memTip: For any 'explain how X is adapted for diffusion' question, write three things: surface area, membrane thickness, and blood supply — and say how each one affects the rate. That three-point structure maps directly onto the marks. Alveoli and villi use exactly the same three adaptations.

tables.title: Factors affecting the rate of diffusion
rows: More particles colliding toward the low-concentration side
More space for particles to cross at once
Particles have more kinetic energy — move faster
Shorter distance particles must travel to cross

1.question: Which of the following is the best definition of diffusion?
options: The movement of water through a partially permeable membrane
The net movement of particles from high to low concentration, requiring no energy
The active transport of dissolved substances against a concentration gradient
The movement of particles in all directions equally
1.correctFeedback: That's it — net movement, high to low, no energy. Three parts, all correct.
1.wrongFeedback: Diffusion is specifically the net movement of particles from high to low concentration, and it's passive — no energy needed. Water through a membrane is osmosis. Movement against a gradient needs active transport.

2.question: A student increases the temperature of a diffusion experiment. What effect does this have?
options: The rate of diffusion decreases because particles slow down
The rate of diffusion stays the same — temperature doesn't affect diffusion
The rate of diffusion increases because particles have more kinetic energy
2.correctFeedback: Correct — more kinetic energy means particles move faster, so the rate increases.
2.wrongFeedback: Higher temperature gives particles more kinetic energy, so they move faster — this increases the rate of diffusion. Temperature is one of the four key factors.

commonMistakes: Saying diffusion 'uses energy' or 'requires ATP' — it doesn't. It's passive. Active transport is the one that needs energy.
Getting the direction wrong — diffusion is always from HIGH to LOW concentration. Never the other way.
Saying 'particles move' without saying 'net movement' — in reality particles move in both directions; what matters is the net (overall) direction.
Forgetting the blood supply adaptation — it's not enough to say alveoli have thin walls and large surface area; the rich blood supply maintains the gradient and is an equally important adaptation.
Mixing up diffusion and osmosis — osmosis is specifically about water; diffusion covers all particles (gases and dissolved substances).
examTips: Any question asking you to 'explain why a structure is adapted for diffusion' needs three things: surface area, membrane thickness, and blood supply — with a reason for each.
When writing the definition, use the word 'net' — without it you might lose a mark. 'Net movement of particles from high to low concentration' is the AQA-approved phrase.
For questions about the body, always specify the direction of diffusion: 'oxygen diffuses FROM the alveoli INTO the blood' — not just 'oxygen diffuses in the lungs'.
revisionCardBullets: Diffusion = net movement of particles from HIGH to LOW concentration — passive, no energy needed
Higher temperature → faster (particles have more kinetic energy)
Thinner membrane → faster (shorter distance to travel)
In lungs: O₂ diffuses from alveoli into blood; CO₂ diffuses from blood into alveoli
In small intestine: glucose and amino acids diffuse from gut into blood
Alveoli adaptations: large surface area, thin walls, rich blood supply
Villi adaptations: large SA (folds + microvilli), thin walls, good blood supply
